references: Tibi P, et al. STS/SCA/AmSECT/SABM Update to the Clinical Practice Guidelines on Patient Blood Management. Ann Thorac Surg 2021;112:981-1004.

questions.stem: Which CPB circuit technique displaces crystalloid prime with the patient's own blood to limit hemodilution and carries a Class I recommendation?
options: Modified ultrafiltration
Retrograde autologous priming
Acute normovolemic hemodilution
Minimally invasive extracorporeal circulation
questions.explanation: Retrograde autologous priming displaces crystalloid prime with the patient's blood and is a Class I recommendation to be used wherever possible. Acute normovolemic hemodilution, MiECC, and modified ultrafiltration are reasonable (Class IIa/IIb) but are not the Class I prime-displacement technique.
references: Tibi P, et al. STS/SCA/AmSECT/SABM Update to the Clinical Practice Guidelines on Patient Blood Management. Ann Thorac Surg 2021;112:981-1004.

questions.stem: Why did lysine analogues replace aprotinin as the default antifibrinolytic in cardiac surgery?
options: Aprotinin was less effective at reducing bleeding than TXA
The BART trial was stopped early for higher mortality with aprotinin versus TXA/EACA
Aprotinin caused an unacceptable rate of seizures
Aprotinin was found to cause heparin resistance
questions.explanation: Aprotinin was actually the most effective antifibrinolytic, but the BART trial (NEJM 2008) was halted early for higher mortality versus TXA/EACA in high-risk cardiac surgery, and the drug was withdrawn, illustrating that efficacy does not equal safety. Seizures are the dose-related concern with TXA, not aprotinin, and aprotinin did not cause heparin resistance.
references: Fergusson DA, et al. A comparison of aprotinin and lysine analogues in high-risk cardiac surgery (BART). N Engl J Med 2008;358:2319-2331.

questions.stem: In the ATACAS trial, tranexamic acid reduced bleeding and transfusion in coronary surgery without increasing death or thrombotic events, but it was associated with an increase in which dose-related complication?
options: Seizures
Acute kidney injury
Myocardial infarction
Deep vein thrombosis
questions.explanation: ATACAS showed TXA cut bleeding and transfusion without raising death or thrombosis, but seizures were increased and are dose-related, motivating use of the lowest effective dose and caution in renal failure and open-chamber cases. It did not increase AKI, MI, or DVT in the trial.
references: Myles PS, et al. Tranexamic acid in patients undergoing coronary-artery surgery (ATACAS). N Engl J Med 2017;376:136-148.

questions.stem: What is the conventional activated clotting time (ACT) target for safe initiation and maintenance of full cardiopulmonary bypass?
options: Greater than 200 seconds
Greater than 300 seconds
Greater than 480 seconds
Greater than 600 seconds
questions.explanation: An ACT greater than 480 seconds is the standard target for full CPB (some centers accept greater than 400 with heparin-level monitoring), rechecked every 30 minutes. About 300 seconds is the off-pump CABG target, and 180-220 seconds is used for cath lab/ECMO.
references: Shore-Lesserson L, et al. STS/SCA/AmSECT/SABM Guidelines: Anticoagulation During CPB. Ann Thorac Surg 2018;105:650.

questions.stem: Which mechanism is the single largest contributor to post-cardiopulmonary-bypass bleeding?
options: Acquired platelet dysfunction
Isolated factor VII deficiency
Hypofibrinogenemia from consumption
Residual heparin effect
questions.explanation: Mechanical shear and circuit contact downregulate platelet GPIb and GPIIb/IIIa, making acquired platelet dysfunction the largest single cause of post-CPB bleeding, exceeding factor deficiency. Hypofibrinogenemia and residual heparin contribute but are not the dominant mechanism, and isolated factor VII deficiency is not a typical CPB phenomenon.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed. Ch 28.

questions.stem: In a bleeding patient after CPB, a ROTEM shows a low FIBTEM A10 with an otherwise reasonable tracing. According to the viscoelastic-guided algorithm, what is the first product to give?
options: Platelets
Fibrinogen concentrate or cryoprecipitate
4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate
Fresh frozen plasma
questions.explanation: A low FIBTEM amplitude isolates the fibrinogen contribution to clot strength, so fibrinogen replacement (concentrate or cryoprecipitate) is the first correction; fibrinogen is typically the first defect to fix in post-CPB bleeding. Platelets, PCC, and FFP are reserved for later algorithm steps targeting platelet contribution or factor deficiency.
references: Tibi P, et al. STS/SCA/AmSECT/SABM PBM Update. Ann Thorac Surg 2021;112:981.

questions.stem: On post-CPB ROTEM, which pattern indicates residual heparin that should be treated with additional protamine?
options: FIBTEM A10 markedly reduced with normal EXTEM
INTEM CT prolonged with a normal HEPTEM CT
EXTEM maximum lysis greater than 15%
EXTEM CT prolonged with normal FIBTEM
questions.explanation: HEPTEM adds heparinase to INTEM; when the INTEM CT is prolonged but the HEPTEM CT corrects to normal, the prolongation is due to residual heparin and more protamine is indicated. A low FIBTEM points to hypofibrinogenemia, high EXTEM ML to hyperfibrinolysis, and a prolonged EXTEM CT with normal FIBTEM to factor deficiency.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed. Ch 28.

questions.stem: A patient is receiving aprotinin. Which ACT activator should be used to avoid a falsely prolonged ACT and apparent over-heparinization?
options: Celite
Kaolin
Glass beads with no activator
Recalcified citrate with no particulate activator
questions.explanation: Aprotinin falsely prolongs the celite-activated ACT far more than the kaolin ACT, so kaolin (or an HMS heparin level) should be used in patients on aprotinin. A celite ACT overstates the heparin effect, an unactivated/recalcified test is too insensitive for CPB-level heparin, and a glass cartridge does not solve the aprotinin interference as reliably as kaolin.
references: Shore-Lesserson L, et al. STS/SCA/AmSECT/SABM Guidelines: Anticoagulation During CPB. Ann Thorac Surg 2018;105:650.

questions.stem: A post-cardiac-surgery patient has a 4T score of 6. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Continue heparin and monitor platelet counts
Stop all heparin and start a non-heparin anticoagulant
Transfuse platelets to correct thrombocytopenia
Begin warfarin monotherapy
questions.explanation: A 4T score of 6-8 indicates high pretest probability (~64%) of HIT, so all heparin should be stopped and a non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban or bivalirudin) started while testing is sent. Continuing heparin is dangerous, platelet transfusion may potentiate thrombosis, and warfarin monotherapy risks venous limb gangrene and skin necrosis without a DTI bridge.
references: Cuker A, et al. American Society of Hematology Guidelines for Management of HIT. Blood Adv 2018.

questions.stem: A patient with acute HIT (positive PF4 antibody and functional assay) needs urgent CPB. Bivalirudin is chosen for anticoagulation. Which pitfall is most important to communicate to the perfusionist and surgical team?
options: Bivalirudin requires protamine reversal before separation
Stagnant blood in the circuit or suction lines will clot because the drug is cleared by proteolysis
Bivalirudin must be avoided in patients with normal renal function
Bivalirudin has no measurable effect on the ACT
questions.explanation: Bivalirudin is cleared by proteolysis and renal elimination, so stagnant blood (cardiotomy reservoir, side pockets, suction lines) will clot; keeping flow through all circuit components is essential. There is no protamine reversal (clearance is the reversal), renal failure prolongs its half-life rather than contraindicating it in normal kidneys, and it does prolong the ACT, targeting approximately 2.5 times baseline.
references: Dyke CM, et al. Bivalirudin for CPB (CHOOSE-ON). Ann Thorac Surg 2007;84:836.

questions.stem: A HIT patient with end-stage renal disease but intact hepatic function requires a direct thrombin inhibitor. Which agent is preferred and why?
options: Bivalirudin, because it is renally cleared and predictable in ESRD
Argatroban, because it is hepatically cleared and unaffected by renal failure
Fondaparinux, because it is safe for CPB anticoagulation
Lepirudin, because it is the current standard for HIT
questions.explanation: Argatroban undergoes hepatic clearance, so it is preferred over bivalirudin in renal failure when hepatic function is intact, since bivalirudin's half-life can triple in ESRD. Fondaparinux has a long half-life and renal clearance unsuitable for CPB, and lepirudin has been withdrawn from the market.
references: Cuker A, et al. American Society of Hematology Guidelines for Management of HIT. Blood Adv 2018.

questions.stem: Cerebral oximetry (near-infrared spectroscopy, NIRS) provides a regional saturation value that predominantly reflects which vascular compartment?
options: Arterial blood only
A mixed sample weighted toward venous blood (approximately 70-75% venous)
Capillary blood only
Cerebrospinal fluid oxygen content
questions.explanation: NIRS measures regional cerebral oxygen saturation from a mixed arterial-venous field weighted roughly 70-75% venous and 25-30% arterial, reflecting the balance of cerebral oxygen supply and demand. It is not a pure arterial, capillary, or CSF measurement.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed.

questions.stem: A processed EEG (BIS) value targeted to maintain general anesthesia and reduce awareness typically falls within which range?
questions.explanation: A BIS value of 40-60 corresponds to an appropriate depth of general anesthesia and is associated with reduced risk of intraoperative awareness. Values near 0 indicate isoelectric EEG/burst suppression, while values of 70-100 indicate light sedation or wakefulness.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed.

questions.stem: During CPB, regional cerebral oxygen saturation by NIRS falls more than 20% below baseline on one side. Which intervention is LEAST likely to correct the desaturation?
options: Increasing arterial PaCO2 toward the higher-normal range
Confirming correct cannula position and head/neck alignment
Raising the mean arterial perfusion pressure
Deepening anesthesia with additional volatile agent
questions.explanation: Deepening anesthesia lowers cerebral metabolic demand but does not address impaired delivery and is the least useful corrective step for an acute desaturation; if anything it does not restore supply. Increasing PaCO2 (cerebral vasodilation), checking cannula position and head alignment (mechanical obstruction of venous drainage or arterial inflow), and raising MAP all directly improve cerebral oxygen delivery.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed.

questions.stem: Somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEPs) monitor the integrity of which pathway, and what is their principal limitation during aortic or spinal surgery?
options: The dorsal column-medial lemniscus pathway; they do not directly monitor anterior (motor) spinal cord function
The corticospinal tract; they cannot detect sensory injury
The spinothalamic tract; they are insensitive to ischemia
The cerebellar pathways; they require muscle relaxation to record
questions.explanation: SSEPs travel in the dorsal column-medial lemniscus (posterior) pathway, so they can miss anterior spinal cord (motor) ischemia in the anterior spinal artery territory, which is why motor evoked potentials are added during thoracoabdominal aortic work. They do not primarily monitor the corticospinal or spinothalamic tracts, and SSEPs (unlike MEPs) are relatively preserved with neuromuscular blockade.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed.

questions.stem: When motor evoked potentials are being monitored during descending thoracic aortic surgery, which anesthetic management choice best preserves recordable signals?
options: High-dose volatile anesthetic (greater than 1 MAC) with continuous neuromuscular blockade
Total intravenous anesthesia with propofol and an opioid, avoiding or minimizing neuromuscular blockade
Nitrous oxide-based technique with deep paralysis
Ketamine infusion with full neuromuscular blockade
questions.explanation: MEPs are highly sensitive to volatile agents and abolished by neuromuscular blockade, so TIVA (propofol plus opioid) with minimal or no paralysis best preserves recordable motor responses. High-dose volatile, nitrous oxide with deep paralysis, and any technique with full neuromuscular blockade suppress MEP signals.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed.

questions.stem: During cooling for deep hypothermic circulatory arrest, EEG is sometimes used to confirm electrocerebral silence before arrest. What does electrocerebral silence indicate in this setting?
options: Maximal suppression of cerebral metabolic activity, suggesting adequate neuroprotection before arrest
Irreversible brain injury has occurred
The patient is inadequately anesthetized
Cerebral perfusion pressure is excessive
questions.explanation: Electrocerebral silence reflects maximal suppression of cerebral electrical and metabolic activity from profound hypothermia, the point at which the brain is best protected for circulatory arrest. It does not indicate irreversible injury, light anesthesia, or excessive perfusion pressure.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed.

questions.stem: Shortly after initiating CPB for an aortic dissection repair, the left cerebral NIRS value drops abruptly and unilaterally while the right remains stable. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Global hypoperfusion from low pump flow
Malposition of the aortic cannula causing selective malperfusion or innominate/carotid involvement by the dissection flap
Anesthetic overdose
A falsely low value from ambient operating room light
questions.explanation: An abrupt unilateral cerebral desaturation strongly suggests selective malperfusion, such as aortic cannula malposition or extension of the dissection flap into the innominate or carotid artery compromising flow to one hemisphere. Global hypoperfusion and anesthetic effects would lower both sides, and ambient light artifact is not typically abrupt and unilateral.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed.

questions.stem: A cardiac surgical patient on a ketamine and nitrous oxide-supplemented anesthetic has an unexpectedly high BIS value despite clinical signs of adequate anesthetic depth. What best explains this discrepancy?
options: Ketamine and nitrous oxide can raise the processed EEG index even at adequate anesthetic depth
BIS always underestimates depth during cardiac surgery
Hypothermia falsely elevates the BIS
Volatile agents reliably correct any BIS artifact
questions.explanation: Ketamine (an NMDA antagonist) and nitrous oxide increase high-frequency EEG activity and can paradoxically raise the BIS value despite adequate anesthesia, a recognized limitation of processed EEG. Hypothermia tends to lower the index, BIS does not uniformly underestimate depth, and volatile agents do not reliably correct these drug-specific artifacts.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed.

questions.stem: What is the primary rationale for using a multimodal analgesic strategy after cardiac surgery?
options: It allows reliance on a single high-dose opioid infusion for predictable analgesia
It targets multiple pain pathways to improve analgesia while reducing opioid requirements and opioid-related side effects
It eliminates the need for any opioid administration in all patients
It is intended primarily to prolong mechanical ventilation for hemodynamic stability
questions.explanation: Multimodal analgesia combines agents acting at different sites (e.g., acetaminophen, gabapentinoids, ketamine, dexmedetomidine, regional blocks) to provide additive or synergistic analgesia while limiting opioid dose and its side effects such as respiratory depression, ileus, and delayed extubation. It reduces rather than eliminates opioid use, and modern cardiac ERAS aims to facilitate earlier extubation, not prolong ventilation.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: Pain from a median sternotomy incision is primarily transmitted by which nerves?
options: The phrenic nerves
The anterior cutaneous branches of the intercostal nerves
The vagus nerves
The long thoracic nerves
questions.explanation: Sensory innervation of the anterior chest wall over the sternum comes from the anterior cutaneous branches of the intercostal nerves (T2-T6), which is why parasternal/pectointercostal fascial-plane blocks target this region. The phrenic nerve is motor to the diaphragm, the vagus is autonomic, and the long thoracic nerve supplies serratus anterior motor function.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A cardiac ERAS program wants to add a fascial-plane block specifically to cover midline sternotomy pain. Which block most directly targets the relevant nerves?
options: Pectointercostal fascial plane (parasternal) block
Adductor canal block
Transversus abdominis plane (TAP) block
Interscalene brachial plexus block
questions.explanation: The pectointercostal fascial plane (parasternal) block deposits local anesthetic between the pectoralis major and the intercostal muscles near the sternal border, anesthetizing the anterior cutaneous branches of the intercostal nerves that supply the sternum. TAP blocks cover the abdominal wall, adductor canal covers the knee, and interscalene covers the shoulder/arm.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: Why has high thoracic epidural analgesia for cardiac surgery remained controversial despite providing excellent analgesia?
options: It provides no measurable reduction in pain scores after sternotomy
Full systemic heparinization for cardiopulmonary bypass raises concern for epidural hematoma
It reliably causes profound hypertension that destabilizes the patient
Local anesthetics are completely ineffective in the thoracic epidural space
questions.explanation: The central safety concern with neuraxial techniques in cardiac surgery is spinal/epidural hematoma because patients receive full-dose heparin for cardiopulmonary bypass shortly after catheter placement. ASRA guidance emphasizes timing of placement relative to anticoagulation. Thoracic epidurals actually lower pain scores and tend to produce sympathectomy-related hypotension, not hypertension.
references: ASRA Guidelines on Regional Anesthesia in the Patient Receiving Antithrombotic/Thrombolytic Therapy, 4th ed.

questions.stem: Which mechanism best explains the analgesic and opioid-sparing benefit of low-dose ketamine as part of multimodal cardiac surgical analgesia?
options: Selective COX-2 inhibition
NMDA receptor antagonism reducing central sensitization
Mu-opioid receptor agonism
Sodium-channel blockade of peripheral nerves
questions.explanation: Ketamine is a noncompetitive NMDA receptor antagonist; by attenuating central sensitization and opioid-induced hyperalgesia it reduces opioid requirements at subanesthetic doses. COX inhibition describes NSAIDs, mu agonism describes opioids, and sodium-channel blockade describes local anesthetics.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old with reduced renal function is recovering from CABG. Why are conventional NSAIDs and COX-2 inhibitors generally used cautiously or avoided in this setting?
options: They reliably cause excessive sedation and delay extubation
They raise the risk of renal dysfunction, bleeding, and adverse cardiovascular/thrombotic events after CABG
They have no analgesic effect after sternotomy
They antagonize the analgesic effect of acetaminophen
questions.explanation: NSAIDs and COX-2 inhibitors are used cautiously after cardiac surgery because of nephrotoxicity (especially with hypovolemia or pre-existing renal impairment), platelet/bleeding effects with nonselective agents, and increased cardiovascular/thrombotic risk demonstrated after CABG. They are effective analgesics and do not sedate or antagonize acetaminophen.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: Which property of dexmedetomidine makes it attractive as an analgesic/sedative adjunct in a fast-track cardiac recovery pathway?
options: It produces sedation and analgesia with minimal respiratory depression
It is a potent NMDA agonist that deepens general anesthesia
It reliably produces tachycardia and hypertension
It must be discontinued before extubation because it always abolishes respiratory drive
questions.explanation: Dexmedetomidine is a central alpha-2 agonist providing sedation, anxiolysis, and opioid-sparing analgesia with relatively preserved respiratory drive, allowing its use during weaning and around extubation. Its typical hemodynamic effects are bradycardia and hypotension, and it is not an NMDA agonist.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: What is the role of scheduled acetaminophen in a multimodal analgesic regimen after cardiac surgery?
options: It is a baseline opioid-sparing analgesic with a favorable side-effect profile when dosed within hepatic limits
It provides profound surgical-level analgesia sufficient as a sole agent
It is contraindicated in all post-CPB patients
It reverses opioid-induced respiratory depression
questions.explanation: Scheduled acetaminophen serves as a foundational, opioid-sparing component of multimodal analgesia, with a generally favorable safety profile when total daily dose respects hepatic limits. It is not a sole analgesic, is not categorically contraindicated post-CPB, and has no opioid-reversal activity (that is naloxone).
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: For analgesia after a minimally invasive lateral thoracotomy or thoracoscopic cardiac approach, an erector spinae plane (ESP) block is chosen. Where is local anesthetic deposited?
options: Into the pleural space directly
Deep to the erector spinae muscle, against the transverse process of the vertebra
Between the pectoralis major and pectoralis minor muscles
Within the epidural space under direct fluoroscopy
questions.explanation: In the ESP block, local anesthetic is injected in the fascial plane deep to the erector spinae muscle overlying the transverse process, spreading to the paravertebral region to block dorsal and ventral rami. It is not an interpleural, pectoral, or epidural injection, which describes different techniques.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A patient with chronic high-dose opioid use is scheduled for sternotomy. Which strategy is most consistent with cardiac ERAS principles for managing this patient's perioperative pain?
options: Withhold all opioids perioperatively to force opioid weaning
Continue baseline opioid requirements and add multimodal/regional adjuncts, anticipating higher total opioid needs
Rely exclusively on a single fascial-plane block without systemic analgesics
Use only an NSAID infusion regardless of renal status
questions.explanation: Opioid-tolerant patients require their baseline opioid maintained to avoid withdrawal, plus aggressive multimodal and regional adjuncts to control incremental surgical pain; their total opioid needs are typically higher, not lower. Abruptly withholding opioids risks withdrawal and uncontrolled pain, a single block is insufficient, and unrestricted NSAID use ignores renal/bleeding risk.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: In the NBG pacemaker code, what does the first letter designate?
options: The chamber(s) sensed
The chamber(s) paced
The response to a sensed event
The rate-modulation feature
questions.explanation: In the NBG code, position I is the chamber(s) paced, position II is the chamber(s) sensed, position III is the response to sensing (inhibited/triggered/dual), and position IV denotes rate modulation. Thus the first letter is the chamber paced.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A DOO pacing mode delivers what type of pacing?
options: Atrial pacing inhibited by sensed atrial activity
Dual-chamber (atrial and ventricular) asynchronous pacing with no sensing
Ventricular pacing triggered only by sensed P waves
Single-chamber ventricular demand pacing
questions.explanation: DOO denotes dual-chamber pacing (atrium and ventricle), no sensing (O in position II), and no response to sensing (O in position III)—i.e., asynchronous AV-sequential pacing. Demand/inhibited modes carry an I in position III, and P-synchronous tracking is described by DDD.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A post-CPB patient in complete heart block has preserved sinus node function and a stiff, hypertrophied left ventricle. Which temporary pacing mode best optimizes cardiac output?
options: VOO ventricular asynchronous pacing
VVI ventricular demand pacing
AV-sequential (DDD or DOO) pacing preserving atrial contribution
AAI atrial demand pacing
questions.explanation: A noncompliant, hypertrophied ventricle is highly dependent on the atrial kick, so AV-sequential pacing that preserves coordinated atrial-then-ventricular contraction maximizes ventricular filling and cardiac output. Pure ventricular pacing (VOO/VVI) loses AV synchrony, and AAI is inappropriate because AV conduction is absent in complete heart block.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: On the monitor, pacing spikes are visible but are not followed by QRS complexes in a patient being ventricularly paced via epicardial wires. What does this finding indicate?
options: Failure to capture
Oversensing
Failure to output
Appropriate demand inhibition
questions.explanation: Pacing spikes that are not followed by depolarization (QRS) define failure to capture, often from rising threshold, lead/wire dysfunction, ischemia, or metabolic derangement; the first maneuver is usually to increase output (mA). Failure to output shows no spikes at all, oversensing causes inappropriate inhibition (missing spikes when expected), and demand inhibition is appropriate suppression by intrinsic beats.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A patient develops intermittent failure to capture on a temporary epicardial ventricular lead set at 5 mA. What is the most appropriate initial response?
options: Switch the generator to a sensing-only mode
Increase the pacing output (mA) above the capture threshold
Decrease the sensitivity setting to a higher mV value
Discontinue pacing and observe
questions.explanation: Failure to capture is addressed first by increasing pacing output (mA) to exceed the (often rising) capture threshold, while also checking connections, polarity, and correcting ischemia or electrolyte abnormalities. Changing sensitivity affects sensing, not capture, and stopping pacing in a pacing-dependent patient is dangerous.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A ventricular demand (VVI) temporary pacemaker is inappropriately inhibited so the patient becomes bradycardic, even though intrinsic ventricular activity is inadequate. Which adjustment most directly corrects oversensing?
options: Decrease the sensitivity by increasing the mV threshold (making the device less sensitive)
Increase the sensitivity by lowering the mV threshold
Increase the pacing output (mA)
Shorten the AV delay
questions.explanation: Oversensing means the device detects extraneous signals (myopotentials, EMI, T waves) as cardiac events and inhibits pacing; making the device less sensitive by raising the sensitivity threshold (higher mV) corrects it. Lowering the mV value makes it more sensitive and worsens the problem; output (mA) governs capture, and AV delay is irrelevant in single-chamber VVI.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A patient dependent on a temporary epicardial pacemaker requires reoperation with electrocautery. What is the most appropriate pacing strategy to prevent EMI-induced inhibition?
options: Set the generator to a demand (VVI/DDD) mode to allow intrinsic beats
Set the generator to an asynchronous mode (VOO/DOO) for the period of cautery use
Lower the pacing rate well below the intrinsic rate
Maximize sensitivity to better detect intrinsic activity
questions.explanation: Electrocautery generates electromagnetic interference that demand-mode generators may misinterpret as intrinsic activity, causing inappropriate inhibition and asystole in a pacing-dependent patient; switching to an asynchronous mode (VOO/DOO) ensures continuous pacing regardless of EMI. Demand modes and increased sensitivity worsen susceptibility to inhibition, and lowering the rate does not address the problem.
references: HRS/ACC Expert Consensus on Perioperative Management of Cardiac Implantable Electronic Devices

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common indication for initiating temporary epicardial pacing immediately after separation from cardiopulmonary bypass?
options: Sinus tachycardia from inadequate anesthesia
Sinus bradycardia or AV block producing inadequate heart rate and low cardiac output
Ventricular fibrillation
Hypertensive urgency
questions.explanation: After CPB, conduction-system edema, cardioplegia effects, surgical trauma, and ischemia commonly produce sinus bradycardia or AV block; pacing restores an adequate rate and AV synchrony to support cardiac output (CO = HR x SV). Tachycardia and hypertension are not pacing indications, and ventricular fibrillation requires defibrillation, not pacing.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A post-CPB patient in sinus rhythm at 50 bpm has a low cardiac index and otherwise adequate filling and contractility. Using atrial pacing to increase the rate toward 80-90 bpm primarily improves cardiac output by which mechanism?
options: Increasing stroke volume through enhanced preload
Increasing heart rate while preserving AV synchrony
Reducing systemic vascular resistance
Augmenting myocardial contractility directly
questions.explanation: Because cardiac output is the product of heart rate and stroke volume, increasing a low heart rate with atrial pacing raises output while preserving normal AV conduction and the atrial contribution to filling. It does not directly enhance contractility or change SVR, and faster rates tend to reduce, not increase, preload/filling time.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: By common surgical convention, temporary epicardial pacing wires brought out through the skin are positioned such that:
options: Atrial wires exit to the patient's left and ventricular wires to the right of the sternum
Atrial wires exit to the patient's right and ventricular wires to the left of the sternum
Both atrial and ventricular wires exit at the same midline site
Wire location is randomized and cannot be inferred from exit site
questions.explanation: By widely used convention, atrial epicardial wires are brought out on the patient's right side of the sternum/epigastrium and ventricular wires on the left, helping the team identify which chamber each set paces; the convention should still be confirmed by capture characteristics and the operative note. The other options reverse, conflate, or deny this convention.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed.

questions.stem: A patient being evaluated before cardiac surgery reports being able to climb a flight of stairs without stopping. Approximately what metabolic equivalent (MET) level does this represent, and what is its usual implication for further cardiac testing?
options: About 4 METs; usually no further cardiac testing is needed
About 1 MET; further stress testing is mandatory
About 10 METs; the patient should be deferred for cardiopulmonary exercise testing
About 2 METs; coronary angiography is required before surgery
questions.explanation: Climbing a flight of stairs corresponds to roughly 4 METs, the pivot of the perioperative algorithm; functional capacity at or above 4 METs (or DASI > 34) usually means no further cardiac testing is required. It is below 4 METs or unknown capacity in an elevated-risk patient that triggers biomarkers and, rarely, stress testing.
references: Thompson A, et al. 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guideline. Circulation. 2024;150(19):e351-e442.

questions.stem: Which single non-invasive test is described as the cornerstone of preoperative evaluation because one study characterizes ventricular function, every valve, pulmonary pressures, and the pericardium?
options: Transthoracic echocardiography
Exercise treadmill ECG
SPECT myocardial perfusion imaging
Coronary CT angiography
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is the single most informative non-invasive test before cardiac surgery, characterizing LV and RV function, all valves, PA systolic pressure from the TR jet, and the pericardium in one study. Stress and nuclear tests answer narrower, specific questions and are not first-line comprehensive structural assessments.
references: Lang RM, et al. Recommendations for Cardiac Chamber Quantification (ASE/EACVI Update). J Am Soc Echocardiogr. 2015;28(1):1-39.

questions.stem: How does slow 5-metre gait speed modify the operative risk estimate derived from the STS-PROM in an older cardiac surgical patient?
options: It adds roughly a 2-3 fold higher risk for the same predicted mortality
It has no independent prognostic value once STS-PROM is calculated
It lowers the predicted risk because it reflects preserved mobility
It only matters in patients younger than 65
questions.explanation: Frailty independently predicts mortality, morbidity, prolonged stay, and loss of independence beyond any risk score; slow gait speed adds a 2-3 times higher risk for the same STS-PROM-predicted mortality. The 5-metre gait speed (slow if > 6 s) is the single best bedside frailty test and corrects what the scores miss.
references: Afilalo J, et al. Frailty in Older Adults Undergoing Cardiac Surgery. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2010;56(20):1668-1676.

questions.stem: In a patient with suspected low-flow, low-gradient aortic stenosis, what finding on dobutamine stress echocardiography indicates TRUE severe stenosis rather than pseudo-severe disease?
options: Valve area stays small while the gradient climbs as flow rises
Valve area increases substantially as flow rises
The gradient falls to normal with augmented flow
The ejection fraction normalizes without a change in valve area
questions.explanation: Dobutamine stress echo distinguishes true severe AS (valve area remains small and the gradient climbs as flow increases) from pseudo-severe AS (valve area increases with augmented flow, revealing a non-critical valve). Contractile reserve assessed during the study also carries prognostic weight.
references: Pellikka PA, et al. Guidelines for Stress Echocardiography. J Am Soc Echocardiogr. 2020;33(1):1-41.

questions.stem: Per the 2024 guidance, a normal preoperative natriuretic peptide level supports a low-risk classification. Which set of values represents the normal cutoffs cited for this purpose?
options: BNP < 92 pg/mL and NT-proBNP < 300 ng/L
BNP < 500 pg/mL and NT-proBNP < 1800 ng/L
BNP < 10 pg/mL and NT-proBNP < 50 ng/L
BNP < 250 pg/mL and NT-proBNP < 900 ng/L
questions.explanation: A normal natriuretic peptide (BNP < 92 pg/mL, NT-proBNP < 300 ng/L) is a commonly cited low-risk cutoff. Preoperative NT-proBNP/BNP carries a Class 2a recommendation specifically for elevated-risk patients with poor or unknown functional capacity, and the optimal threshold is not firmly established.
references: Thompson A, et al. 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guideline. Circulation. 2024;150(19):e351-e442.

questions.stem: A preoperative TTE shows severe aortic stenosis. Which combination of intraoperative hemodynamic goals best matches this lesion?
options: Maintain sinus rhythm and preload, preserve SVR, and avoid tachycardia and hypotension
Promote tachycardia and aggressive afterload reduction to improve forward flow
Encourage low SVR and permissive hypotension to unload the ventricle
Induce controlled bradycardia with vasodilation to lengthen filling
questions.explanation: Severe AS demands maintenance of sinus rhythm (atrial kick is critical for the stiff, hypertrophied LV), adequate preload, and preserved SVR to sustain coronary perfusion pressure; tachycardia and hypotension are poorly tolerated. Afterload reduction and permissive hypotension can precipitate catastrophic coronary hypoperfusion in fixed obstruction.
references: Otto CM, et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for Valvular Heart Disease. Circulation. 2021;143(5):e72-e227.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old with EF 35%, LBBB on ECG, and a question of myocardial viability needs ischemia and viability assessment before possible revascularization. Why is an exercise treadmill ECG a poor choice, and what modality better answers the viability question?
options: LBBB and inability to exercise make the ECG uninterpretable/unfeasible; PET or late-gadolinium CMR better assesses viability
Exercise ECG is ideal here because LBBB enhances ST-segment interpretation
Exercise ECG is preferred and viability cannot be assessed by any imaging test
A resting plain chest radiograph is the modality of choice for viability
questions.explanation: LBBB renders exercise-ECG ST analysis uninterpretable, and a patient who cannot achieve adequate workload cannot generate a diagnostic study. Viability is best assessed with PET, dobutamine echo, or late-gadolinium CMR, which predict which segments recover function after revascularization, important guidance with an EF of 35%.
references: Thompson A, et al. 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guideline. Circulation. 2024;150(19):e351-e442.

questions.stem: A patient with prior CABG presents for redo cardiac surgery. Which preoperative imaging study is specifically indicated to plan the anesthetic and surgical approach, and what is its main purpose?
options: CT chest to map RV, graft, and aortic proximity to the sternum and plan peripheral cannulation
Carotid ultrasound to determine cannulation strategy
Nuclear MPI to estimate the risk of sternal re-entry injury
Pulmonary function testing to map graft anatomy
questions.explanation: Before redo sternotomy, CT chest maps the proximity of the RV, patent grafts, and aorta to the posterior sternal table, informing the decision for peripheral (femoral) cannulation, readiness of external defibrillator pads, and cross-matched blood. Carotid ultrasound, MPI, and PFTs do not define re-entry risk anatomy.
references: Thompson A, et al. 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guideline. Circulation. 2024;150(19):e351-e442.

questions.stem: When comparing cardiac CT and cardiac MRI, which statement correctly pairs each modality with its principal strength?
options: CT is best for coronary anatomy and structural sizing; MRI is best for tissue characterization and scar
CT is best for myocardial scar and viability; MRI is best for calcium scoring
CT requires gadolinium; MRI requires iodinated contrast
CT is radiation-free; MRI delivers 10-15 mSv per study
questions.explanation: Cardiac CT excels at coronary anatomy, structural sizing, calcium, and access planning, while CMR is the reference for tissue characterization, scar, viability, and ventricular volumes. CT uses iodinated contrast and ionizing radiation; MRI uses gadolinium and no radiation.
references: Hundley WG, et al. ACCF/ACR/AHA expert consensus on CMR. JACC. 2010;55:2614-2662.

questions.stem: What is the target heart rate for diagnostic-quality coronary CT angiography, and which first-line agents are used to achieve it?
options: Sinus rate at or below 60 bpm, achieved with beta-blockers such as metoprolol
Heart rate above 90 bpm, achieved with atropine
Heart rate of 75-85 bpm, achieved with ivabradine alone
Heart rate is irrelevant to coronary CTA image quality
questions.explanation: Coronary anatomy is reconstructed from mid-diastasis, a window that shortens nonlinearly above 65 bpm, so sinus at or below 60 bpm is the diagnostic target. Beta-blockade (oral then IV metoprolol) is first-line, with SL nitroglycerin for coronary dilation; ivabradine is the alternative when beta-blockers are contraindicated.
references: Mahabadi AA, et al. Heart rate and image quality in CCTA. Eur Heart J. 2010;31:2078-2086.

questions.stem: A patient referred for coronary CTA has severe asthma and a heart rate of 78 bpm in sinus rhythm. Beta-blockers are contraindicated. Which rate-control agent is best suited because it lacks negative inotropy and does not cause bronchospasm?
options: Ivabradine
Esmolol infusion
Inhaled albuterol
Atropine
questions.explanation: Ivabradine is a funny-channel (If) inhibitor that lowers sinus rate without negative inotropy or bronchospasm, making it the preferred alternative when beta-blockers are off the table (e.g., severe asthma). Esmolol is still a beta-blocker; albuterol raises heart rate; atropine increases it as well.
references: Guaricci AI, et al. Ivabradine for pre-CCTA HR control. Int J Cardiol. 2018;272:24-29.

questions.stem: On a pre-TAVR CT report, a low coronary ostial height is flagged as a risk factor. Below what threshold does coronary height raise the concern of coronary occlusion during valve deployment, potentially requiring coronary protection or BASILICA?
options: Less than 12 mm
Less than 30 mm
Less than 25 mm
Less than 20 mm
questions.explanation: Coronary ostial heights below 12 mm carry occlusion risk during TAVR and may prompt a coronary protection wire or BASILICA leaflet laceration. This finding pushes toward GA setup readiness and a surgical bailout plan, distinct from annular sizing (rupture vs PVL) and iliofemoral access (< 5-5.5 mm).
references: Carroll JD, et al. STS/ACC TVT Registry. JACC. 2021;78:e187-e285.

questions.stem: A CMR report describes late gadolinium enhancement that begins at the endocardium. What does this distribution indicate about the underlying process?
options: An ischemic (infarct) etiology, since ischemic LGE always starts subendocardially
A non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, since these always begin at the endocardium
Acute myocarditis, which is exclusively subendocardial
A purely artifactual finding with no clinical meaning
questions.explanation: Ischemic LGE always begins at the subendocardium and extends outward in a coronary territory; subendocardial-only enhancement is viable, while transmurality > 50% predicts non-recovery after revascularization. Non-ischemic patterns (midwall septal stripe in DCM, subepicardial in myocarditis, diffuse in amyloid) characteristically spare the endocardium.
references: Kim RJ, et al. LGE and viability. NEJM. 2000;343:1445-1453.

questions.stem: A preoperative CMR shows diffuse subendocardial-to-transmural LGE, elevated native T1, and an extracellular volume (ECV) greater than 40%. This signature is most consistent with which diagnosis, and what anesthetic vulnerability should be anticipated?
options: Cardiac amyloidosis; vasoplegia-prone with severe diastolic dysfunction and orthostatic intolerance
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; dynamic LVOT obstruction relieved by vasodilators
Acute myocarditis; expect rapid recovery with no rhythm risk
ARVC; isolated RV failure with preserved LV filling
questions.explanation: Diffuse LGE with high native T1 and ECV > 40% is the CMR signature of cardiac amyloid, which produces severe diastolic dysfunction, vasoplegia-proneness, orthostatic intolerance, and poor tolerance of PVCs. HCM shows asymmetric septal hypertrophy, and vasodilators worsen (not relieve) its dynamic obstruction.
references: Fontana M, et al. ECV and T1 in amyloidosis. Circulation. 2015;132:1570-1579.

questions.stem: Following the 2017 HRS pathway, a pacemaker-dependent patient with a non-MR-conditional device requires a clinically necessary 1.5-T cardiac MRI. Which set of steps reflects the consensus protocol supported by the MagnaSafe Registry?
options: EP interrogation pre-scan, reprogram to asynchronous mode, disable ICD shock therapy, continuous monitoring, and re-interrogate after
Place a magnet over the device and proceed without interrogation or monitoring
Cancel the scan because no non-MR-conditional device can ever be scanned
Reprogram to demand mode and remove all monitoring to avoid artifact
questions.explanation: The 2017 HRS pathway calls for pre-scan EP interrogation, asynchronous (DOO/VOO) reprogramming for pacemaker-dependent patients, disabling ICD shock therapy, continuous monitoring during the scan, and post-scan re-interrogation to restore baseline programming. In the MagnaSafe Registry, 0 of roughly 1,500 non-MR-conditional devices failed at 1.5 T under this protocol.
references: Russo RJ, et al. MagnaSafe Registry. NEJM. 2017;376:755-764.

questions.stem: An anesthetized patient in the MRI bore develops cardiac arrest. According to the magnet-environment principles, what is the correct first action?
options: Pull the patient out of the bore and out of Zone IV, then begin resuscitation with the non-ferrous cart
Immediately quench the magnet before touching the patient
Bring the standard resuscitation cart into the scanner room to start CPR in the bore
Begin chest compressions inside the bore while the scan continues
questions.explanation: Most arrests are managed by pulling the patient out of the bore (achievable within about 30 s) and out of Zone IV, where the non-ferrous resuscitation cart waits immediately outside; standard ferromagnetic equipment must never enter the scanner room. Quenching is reserved only for the rare case of a patient trapped against the magnet.
references: Indik JH, et al. 2017 HRS Expert Consensus on MR in CIED patients. Heart Rhythm. 2017;14:e97-e153.

questions.stem: As a pulmonary artery catheter is advanced, the waveform shows a systolic peak similar to the prior chamber, but now the diastolic pressure steps UP and a dicrotic notch appears. Where is the catheter tip?
options: Pulmonary artery
Right atrium
Right ventricle
Pulmonary capillary wedge position
questions.explanation: The PA tracing shares the systolic peak of the RV but its diastolic pressure steps up (and runs off after a dicrotic notch from pulmonic valve closure), distinguishing it from the RV whose diastole is near zero and rises with filling. The RA undulates with a/v waves, and the wedge is damped and low.
references: Kern MJ, Sorajja P, Lim MJ. The Cardiac Catheterization Handbook. 7th ed. Elsevier; 2020.

questions.stem: During an oximetry run, normal right-sided saturations are approximately 70% and left-sided approximately 98%. An unexpected step-up in saturation on the right side most likely indicates what?
options: A left-to-right shunt
A right-to-left shunt
Severe anemia
Carbon monoxide poisoning
questions.explanation: Oxygenated blood added to the right side raises the saturation at and beyond the chamber where the shunt enters, so a right-sided step-up signals a left-to-right shunt. A right-to-left shunt instead desaturates the left side; anemia and CO poisoning affect oxygen content/measurement, not a localized step-up.
references: Ragosta M. Textbook of Clinical Hemodynamics. 2nd ed. Elsevier; 2018.

questions.stem: A wedge tracing shows a giant v wave. What does this finding most strongly suggest, and how does it affect interpretation of the wedge pressure?
options: Severe mitral regurgitation; the wedge overestimates mean left atrial pressure
Severe aortic stenosis; the wedge underestimates LVEDP
Complete heart block; the wedge reads alveolar pressure
Tricuspid stenosis; the wedge equals true LVEDP
questions.explanation: A giant v wave reflects regurgitant volume filling a closed atrium, the signature of severe mitral regurgitation when seen in the wedge (or severe TR when seen in the RA). With a large v wave the mean wedge overestimates mean left atrial pressure, a recognized pitfall of using the wedge as a surrogate.
references: Nishimura RA, Carabello BA. Hemodynamics in the Cardiac Catheterization Laboratory. Circulation. 2012;125(17):2138-2150.

questions.stem: In a patient with severe tricuspid regurgitation and a low cardiac output, which method of measuring cardiac output is most reliable, and why?
options: Fick, because thermodilution is inaccurate with TR, shunts, and low-output states
Thermodilution, because it is unaffected by regurgitation
Thermodilution, because TR speeds the indicator transit
Neither method can be used in the presence of any valve disease
questions.explanation: Thermodilution fails with tricuspid regurgitation, intracardiac shunts, and low-output states because the cold indicator recirculates and distorts the temperature curve. The Fick method (oxygen consumption divided by the arteriovenous oxygen difference) is preferred in low-output and regurgitant physiology; the two methods fail in opposite directions.
references: Ragosta M. Textbook of Clinical Hemodynamics. 2nd ed. Elsevier; 2018.

questions.stem: Using the Fick principle, calculate cardiac output for a patient with an oxygen consumption of 250 mL/min, arterial O2 content of 20 mL/dL, and mixed venous O2 content of 15 mL/dL.
options: 5.0 L/min
2.5 L/min
10.0 L/min
0.5 L/min
questions.explanation: Fick CO = VO2 / (CaO2 - CvO2) with contents expressed per liter. The a-v difference is 20 - 15 = 5 mL/dL = 50 mL/L, so CO = 250 mL/min / 50 mL/L = 5.0 L/min. Forgetting to convert mL/dL to mL/L (a factor of 10) is the classic error producing the 0.5 L/min distractor.
references: Ragosta M. Textbook of Clinical Hemodynamics. 2nd ed. Elsevier; 2018.

questions.stem: An intermediate coronary stenosis is interrogated with a pressure wire. Which thresholds define a flow-limiting lesion that warrants revascularization?
options: FFR at or below 0.80 (hyperemic) or iFR at or below 0.89 (resting)
FFR at or below 0.50 or iFR at or below 0.60
FFR at or above 0.90 or iFR at or above 0.95
FFR at or below 0.99 or iFR at or below 0.99
questions.explanation: Fractional flow reserve, measured under adenosine-induced hyperemia, is significant at 0.80 or below; the resting instantaneous wave-free ratio (iFR) is significant at 0.89 or below. Deferring revascularization of lesions above these thresholds is safe and avoids unnecessary intervention.
references: Tonino PAL, et al. (FAME). Fractional Flow Reserve versus Angiography for Guiding PCI. N Engl J Med. 2009;360(3):213-224.

questions.stem: On a right atrial tracing, which jugular venous/atrial waveform pattern best distinguishes cardiac tamponade from constrictive pericarditis?
options: Tamponade blunts or abolishes the y descent; constriction shows steep x AND y (M/W pattern)
Tamponade shows steep x and y; constriction abolishes the y descent
Both abolish the x descent while preserving y
Tamponade produces cannon a waves; constriction produces giant v waves
questions.explanation: In tamponade, elevated pericardial pressure prevents early diastolic filling, so the y descent is blunted or absent while the x descent is preserved. Constriction permits abrupt early filling that halts suddenly, producing steep x AND y descents (the M/W pattern) with Kussmaul's sign.
references: Geske JB, et al. Differentiation of Constriction and Restriction. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2016;68(21):2329-2347.

questions.stem: During a complete oximetry run, which oxygen-saturation step-up thresholds are considered significant for localizing a left-to-right shunt at the atrial and the ventricular/great-vessel levels, respectively?
options: At least 7% at the atrial level and at least 5% at the ventricular/great-vessel level
At least 2% at both levels
At least 15% at the atrial level and 10% at the ventricular level
At least 5% at the atrial level and 7% at the ventricular level
questions.explanation: A significant step-up is at least 7% at the atrial level and at least 5% at the ventricular or great-vessel level; a screening SVC-to-PA rise greater than 7% prompts a full run. A resulting Qp/Qs greater than 1.5 indicates a hemodynamically significant shunt.
references: Kern MJ, Sorajja P, Lim MJ. The Cardiac Catheterization Handbook. 7th ed. Elsevier; 2020.

questions.stem: What is the recommended perioperative handling of chronic statin therapy in a patient presenting for cardiac surgery?
options: Continue without interruption (Class I)
Hold on the morning of surgery and resume postoperatively
Stop 5 days before surgery to reduce bleeding
Hold 24 hours before surgery to avoid vasoplegia
questions.explanation: Statins should be continued without interruption (Class I); they stabilize plaque and continuation lowers postoperative atrial fibrillation and mortality, and they should never be stopped around cardiac surgery. Holding or stopping statins offers no benefit and removes a protective effect.
references: Thompson A, et al. 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guideline. Circulation. 2024;150:e351-e442.

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects perioperative management of beta-blockers in a patient already on chronic therapy before cardiac surgery?
options: Continue them, including the morning dose, but never start a new beta-blocker on the day of surgery
Hold them the morning of surgery to prevent intraoperative bradycardia
Start a new beta-blocker the morning of surgery in every patient
Stop them 5 days before surgery to prevent rebound
questions.explanation: Chronic beta-blockers are continued (Class I), including the morning dose, because abrupt withdrawal causes rebound tachycardia, hypertension, and ischemia; continuation also lowers postoperative AF after cardiac surgery. The POISE trial showed harm (stroke, death) from initiating a beta-blocker on the day of surgery, so new therapy should begin at least 7 days out and be titrated.
references: Devereaux PJ, et al. (POISE). Lancet. 2008;371:1839-1847.

questions.stem: A patient on empagliflozin for heart failure is scheduled for elective cardiac surgery. What is the 2024 Class I recommendation, and what complication is it intended to prevent?
options: Stop the SGLT2 inhibitor 3-4 days before surgery to prevent euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis
Continue it through the morning of surgery to prevent hyperkalemia
Stop it 24 hours before surgery to prevent vasoplegia
Double the dose preoperatively to prevent hyperglycemia
questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors should be stopped 3-4 days before surgery (Class I, 2024) to prevent euglycemic DKA, in which glucosuria plus fasting and surgical stress produce ketoacidosis with a near-normal glucose that is easily missed. These agents are now prescribed for HF and CKD, not just diabetes, so every patient should be screened.
references: Thompson A, et al. 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guideline. Circulation. 2024;150:e351-e442.

questions.stem: For a patient awaiting elective CABG, which set of P2Y12 inhibitor washout intervals is correct?
options: Clopidogrel 5 days, ticagrelor 3 days, prasugrel 7 days
Clopidogrel 3 days, ticagrelor 5 days, prasugrel 5 days
Clopidogrel 7 days, ticagrelor 7 days, prasugrel 3 days
Clopidogrel 1 day, ticagrelor 1 day, prasugrel 2 days
questions.explanation: Before CABG, clopidogrel is stopped 5 days out (irreversible block; 5 days clears the platelet pool), ticagrelor 3 days (reversible binding with a shorter washout), and prasugrel 7 days (most potent, longest platelet recovery, highest bleed risk). Aspirin is continued through CABG.
references: Sousa-Uva M, et al. 2017 EACTS Guidelines on perioperative medication in adult cardiac surgery. Eur J Cardiothorac Surg. 2018;53:5-33.

questions.stem: An ACE inhibitor prescribed for well-controlled hypertension (not for heart failure) is on a cardiac surgical patient's medication list. What is the recommended handling, and what post-CPB risk is it meant to mitigate?
options: Hold it about 24 hours before surgery to reduce post-CPB vasoplegic syndrome (Class IIb)
Continue it through induction because withdrawal causes rebound vasoplegia
Stop it 7 days before surgery to allow full RAAS recovery
Switch it to an ARNI the night before surgery
questions.explanation: When an ACEi/ARB is prescribed for hypertension with well-controlled BP, holding it about 24 hours before surgery is reasonable (Class IIb), and the vasoplegia concern on CPB is greatest in this group. When the drug is part of HFrEF GDMT, it is reasonable to continue (Class IIa) while preparing for post-bypass vasoplegia with early vasopressin and consideration of methylene blue.
references: Thompson A, et al. 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guideline. Circulation. 2024;150:e351-e442.

questions.stem: Using the PAUSE schedule, how should apixaban be interrupted before a high-bleed-risk cardiac surgery in a patient with normal renal function, and should bridging be used?
options: Skip 2 days (about 48 hours) before surgery, with no bridging
Skip only the morning dose, with LMWH bridging
Continue through surgery and reverse with andexanet at incision
Stop 7 days before surgery and bridge with heparin
questions.explanation: PAUSE validated skipping the DOAC for 2 days (about 48 hours) before high-bleed-risk surgery and restarting 48-72 hours postoperatively, without bridging, since DOACs have a predictable offset and bridging adds bleeding without benefit. Dabigatran with renal impairment (CrCl < 50) requires a longer hold of 4 or more days.
references: Douketis JD, et al. (PAUSE). JAMA Intern Med. 2019;179:1469-1478.

questions.stem: A patient on warfarin for nonvalvular atrial fibrillation with a low-to-moderate CHA2DS2-VASc score needs elective surgery. Based on the BRIDGE trial, what is the recommended periprocedural approach?
options: Stop warfarin about 5 days before surgery and do NOT bridge with heparin
Bridge with therapeutic LMWH because all AF patients require bridging
Continue warfarin through surgery without interruption
Stop warfarin the day before surgery and give andexanet alfa
questions.explanation: Warfarin is stopped about 5 days before surgery; in atrial fibrillation, the BRIDGE trial showed that forgoing bridging was non-inferior for thromboembolism and caused less bleeding, so routine low-to-moderate-risk AF should not be bridged. Bridging is reserved for high-risk patients such as mechanical mitral valves, recent VTE (< 3 months), or prior thromboembolism on interruption.
references: Douketis JD, et al. (BRIDGE). N Engl J Med. 2015;373:823-833.

questions.stem: A patient takes a once-weekly GLP-1 receptor agonist. What is the primary perioperative concern driving its management, and how is it typically handled?
options: Delayed gastric emptying with aspiration risk; hold the weekly agent about 1 week before surgery
Euglycemic ketoacidosis; stop it 3-4 days before surgery
Rebound hypertension; continue it through the morning of surgery
Lactic acidosis; hold only on the day of surgery
questions.explanation: GLP-1 receptor agonists delay gastric emptying and raise aspiration risk, so per consensus protocols daily agents are held on the day of surgery and weekly agents are held about 1 week before. Euglycemic DKA and the 3-4 day hold belong to SGLT2 inhibitors; lactic acidosis with a day-of hold belongs to metformin.
references: ASA. Preoperative management of patients on GLP-1 receptor agonists - consensus guidance, updated multisociety statement 2024.

questions.stem: By 2020 ACC/AHA criteria, which set of hemodynamic findings defines severe aortic stenosis?
options: Vmax 2.0-3.9 m/s, mean gradient <40 mmHg, AVA >1.5 cm²
Vmax ≥4 m/s, mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, AVA ≤1.0 cm²
Vmax ≥3 m/s, mean gradient ≥30 mmHg, AVA ≤1.5 cm²
Vmax <2 m/s, mean gradient <20 mmHg, AVA ≥2.0 cm²
questions.explanation: Severe AS is defined by Vmax ≥4 m/s, mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, and aortic valve area ≤1.0 cm². The lower thresholds describe at-risk (Stage A) or mild-moderate (Stage B) disease.
references: Otto CM et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guideline. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227.

questions.stem: Which combination of hemodynamic goals is most appropriate during induction of a patient with severe aortic stenosis?
options: Low afterload, fast heart rate, permissive hypotension
Maintained afterload, sinus rhythm, heart rate 60-80
High heart rate, reduced preload, vasodilation
Bradycardia, reduced afterload, junctional rhythm acceptable
questions.explanation: Severe AS requires maintaining afterload (diastolic coronary perfusion of the hypertrophied wall), preserving sinus rhythm (atrial kick provides 30-40% of CO), and a heart rate of 60-80. Tachycardia shortens filling and coronary perfusion; hypotension causes subendocardial ischemia.
references: Otto CM et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guideline. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227.

questions.stem: An echo shows AVA 0.9 cm², Vmax 3.5 m/s, mean gradient 32 mmHg, EF 60%, and stroke volume index 30 mL/m² with a small concentric LV. Which AS stage does this represent?
options: D1 - symptomatic high-gradient severe AS
D2 - symptomatic low-flow low-gradient with reduced EF
D3 - paradoxical low-flow low-gradient with preserved EF
B - moderate AS
questions.explanation: Stage D3 is paradoxical low-flow low-gradient severe AS: AVA ≤1.0 cm², Vmax <4, gradient <40, but with preserved EF ≥50% and a low SVi (<35 mL/m²) from a small, stiff concentric LV. D2 requires EF <50%, and D1 requires a high gradient.
references: Otto CM et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guideline. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227.

questions.stem: A patient with asymptomatic severe AS and normal EF presents for hip surgery. Regarding neuraxial anesthesia, which statement is most accurate?
options: Single-shot spinal is preferred because it avoids airway manipulation
Single-shot spinal is best avoided (relatively contraindicated) due to abrupt sympathectomy and afterload drop
Spinal and general anesthesia carry identical hemodynamic risk
Neuraxial of any type is absolutely contraindicated
questions.explanation: A single-shot spinal is best avoided in severe AS because the sudden sympathectomy can precipitously drop afterload, threatening coronary perfusion of the hypertrophied LV. A slowly titrated epidural or CSE with an arterial line and vasopressor is the safer neuraxial approach; neuraxial is not absolutely contraindicated.
references: Thompson A et al. 2024 AHA/ACC perioperative cardiovascular guideline. JACC 2024;84:1869-1969.

questions.stem: A patient with acute severe aortic regurgitation from endocarditis is in pulmonary edema with HR 115. Which intervention is contraindicated?
options: Afterload reduction with nicardipine
Inotropic support with dobutamine
Intra-aortic balloon pump placement
Allowing the heart rate to remain elevated
questions.explanation: An IABP augments diastolic aortic pressure, which increases the regurgitant fraction and worsens forward flow in significant AR; it is contraindicated. Afterload reduction, inotropy, and a faster rate ('fast, full, forward') are all appropriate bridges to surgical AVR.
references: Krishnamoorthy A et al. IABP in valvular disease - contraindications. Curr Cardiol Rep 2014;16:481.

questions.stem: Per the 2020 ACC/AHA guideline, what LVEF threshold in an asymptomatic patient with chronic severe AR is a Class I indication for aortic valve surgery?
options: LVEF ≤35%
LVEF ≤40%
LVEF ≤55%
LVEF ≤60%
questions.explanation: The 2020 guideline lowered the surgical EF trigger from 50% to ≤55% based on data showing late mortality once EF drops below 55%. Asymptomatic severe AR with LVEF ≤55% is a Class I indication for surgery.
references: Otto CM et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guideline. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old with a bicuspid aortic valve is undergoing SAVR for severe AS. The CT shows an ascending aortic diameter of 4.6 cm. Per the 2022 ACC/AHA aortic guideline, what is the appropriate management of the ascending aorta?
options: Leave the aorta; replace only at ≥5.5 cm regardless of concomitant surgery
Replace the ascending aorta because it is ≥4.5 cm with concomitant valve surgery
Defer; reimage in 5 years
Replace only if the diameter exceeds 6.0 cm
questions.explanation: With a BAV, the isolated ascending aneurysm is replaced at ≥5.5 cm (or ≥5.0 cm at experienced aortic centers or with high-risk features), and at ≥4.5 cm when the patient is already undergoing aortic valve surgery. At 4.6 cm with concomitant SAVR, the aorta should be replaced rather than left behind.
references: Isselbacher EM et al. 2022 ACC/AHA Aortic Disease Guideline. Circulation 2022;146:e334-e482.

questions.stem: Which post-procedural complication is more common after TAVR than after surgical AVR, and at what approximate rate?
options: Stroke, at 15-20%
Permanent pacemaker for complete heart block, at 10-15%
Annular rupture, at 25%
Vasoplegia, at 50%
questions.explanation: Complete heart block requiring a permanent pacemaker occurs in roughly 10-15% after TAVR versus 5-8% after SAVR, owing to the conduction system's course along the membranous septum adjacent to the non-coronary cusp. Stroke (2-5%) and annular rupture are far less frequent.
references: Mangieri A et al. Conduction abnormalities after TAVR. JACC Cardiovasc Interv 2018;11:1503-1511.

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of mitral stenosis worldwide?
options: Mitral annular calcification
Rheumatic heart disease
Infective endocarditis
Congenital parachute mitral valve
questions.explanation: Rheumatic heart disease remains the leading cause of mitral stenosis worldwide, producing commissural fusion and leaflet thickening. Mitral annular calcification is an increasingly recognized degenerative cause in the elderly but is far less common overall.
references: Otto CM et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guideline. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227.

questions.stem: Which hemodynamic goal is most important in a patient with severe mitral stenosis?
options: Maintain tachycardia to increase cardiac output
Avoid tachycardia to preserve diastolic filling time
Aggressively reduce afterload
Promote junctional rhythm
questions.explanation: In mitral stenosis, LV filling occurs across a fixed obstruction during diastole; tachycardia shortens diastolic filling time and raises left atrial pressure, precipitating pulmonary edema. Maintaining a controlled rate and sinus rhythm (preserving atrial kick) is essential.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed. Elsevier.

questions.stem: By 2020 ACC/AHA criteria, which mitral valve area defines severe mitral stenosis?
options: MVA ≤2.5 cm²
MVA ≤2.0 cm²
MVA ≤1.5 cm²
MVA ≤1.0 cm²
questions.explanation: Severe mitral stenosis is defined by a mitral valve area ≤1.5 cm² (very severe is ≤1.0 cm²). An MVA between 1.5 and 2.5 cm² represents progressive disease.
references: Otto CM et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guideline. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227.

questions.stem: Which finding is a contraindication to percutaneous balloon mitral commissurotomy in a patient with severe rheumatic mitral stenosis?
options: A favorable Wilkins score of 6
Left atrial appendage thrombus
Mild mitral regurgitation
Sinus rhythm
questions.explanation: Left atrial (or LA appendage) thrombus is a contraindication to percutaneous balloon mitral commissurotomy because of embolic risk, as is moderate-to-severe MR. A low Wilkins score (≤8) and pliable, non-calcified leaflets favor the procedure.
references: Otto CM et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guideline. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227.

questions.stem: Which echocardiographic parameter is consistent with severe chronic primary mitral regurgitation?
options: Effective regurgitant orifice area 0.10 cm²
Regurgitant fraction 30%
Regurgitant volume ≥60 mL with EROA ≥0.40 cm²
Vena contracta width 0.2 cm
questions.explanation: Severe primary MR is defined by EROA ≥0.40 cm², regurgitant volume ≥60 mL, regurgitant fraction ≥50%, and vena contracta ≥0.7 cm. The other values describe mild-to-moderate regurgitation.
references: Zoghbi WA et al. ASE recommendations for native valvular regurgitation. J Am Soc Echocardiogr 2017;30:303-371.

questions.stem: For induction of a patient with severe chronic mitral regurgitation, which set of hemodynamic goals is most appropriate?
options: Increase afterload, maintain bradycardia, restrict volume
Reduce afterload, maintain a normal-to-slightly-elevated heart rate, avoid bradycardia
Maximize afterload with phenylephrine, allow junctional rhythm
Deliberate hypovolemia and high SVR
questions.explanation: MR physiology favors afterload reduction (to promote forward flow over regurgitant flow) and avoidance of bradycardia, which would increase LV volume and regurgitant fraction. Increasing SVR or inducing bradycardia worsens the regurgitant fraction.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed. Elsevier.

questions.stem: Immediately after mitral valve repair with an annuloplasty ring, TEE shows new systolic anterior motion of the mitral leaflet, LVOT obstruction, and posteriorly directed MR. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Start an epinephrine infusion and reduce afterload
Volume loading, increase afterload, and reduce inotropy/heart rate
Place an intra-aortic balloon pump
Begin nitroglycerin and diuresis
questions.explanation: Systolic anterior motion (SAM) is treated by increasing LV volume (preload), increasing afterload, and reducing contractility and heart rate, which enlarge the LV cavity and relieve the dynamic LVOT obstruction. Inotropes, vasodilators, or an IABP worsen SAM. Persistent SAM may require surgical revision.
references: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia, 8th ed. Elsevier.

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure (EF 30%) and severe secondary mitral regurgitation remains symptomatic on guideline-directed medical therapy. Based on the COAPT trial and the 2020 guideline, which intervention carries a Class IIa recommendation?
options: Transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (TEER)
Immediate surgical mitral valve replacement
Percutaneous balloon mitral commissurotomy
Discontinuation of beta-blockade
questions.explanation: For appropriately selected patients with severe secondary MR and reduced EF who remain symptomatic on optimal GDMT, transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (MitraClip) is a Class IIa recommendation, supported by the COAPT trial. Commissurotomy treats stenosis, not regurgitation, and surgery has not shown clear survival benefit in this population.
references: Otto CM et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guideline. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227.

questions.stem: Which organism is now the leading cause of infective endocarditis in the developed world?
options: Viridans group streptococci
Staphylococcus aureus
Enterococcus faecalis
Coagulase-negative staphylococci
questions.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus has overtaken viridans streptococci as the most common cause of IE in the developed world, reflecting more healthcare-associated, IVDU, and prosthetic/device-related disease. S. aureus IE is acute and aggressive with rapid valve destruction and high embolic risk.
references: Fowler VG et al. The 2023 Duke-ISCVID Criteria for Infective Endocarditis. Clin Infect Dis 2023;77:518-526.

questions.topic: Most common surgical indication in IE
questions.stem: What is the most common indication for surgery in infective endocarditis?
options: Heart failure from acute valvular regurgitation
Prevention of embolism
Persistent positive blood cultures alone
Prosthetic valve dehiscence
questions.explanation: Heart failure from acute severe regurgitation (or valve obstruction/fistula) is the leading and most time-sensitive surgical indication. Acute regurgitation onto an unprepared ventricle does not respond to medical therapy the way chronic lesions do, often converting an urgent case into an emergent one.
references: Delgado V et al. 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of endocarditis. Eur Heart J 2023;44:3948-4042.

questions.topic: New AV block in aortic IE
questions.stem: A patient with aortic valve endocarditis develops new first-degree then high-grade AV block. What does this finding most strongly suggest?
options: Coexistent Lyme carditis
Aortic root/perivalvular abscess involving the septum
Digoxin toxicity
Beta-blocker effect
questions.explanation: New or worsening AV block in aortic valve IE strongly suggests perivalvular extension with an aortic-root/septal abscess affecting the adjacent conduction system. This is an indication for surgery (uncontrolled infection) and predicts conduction block that may persist off bypass.
references: Delgado V et al. 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of endocarditis. Eur Heart J 2023;44:3948-4042.

questions.topic: Surgical timing after stroke in IE
questions.stem: A patient with mitral IE and acute severe MR develops a small, asymptomatic embolic ischemic infarct (no hemorrhage) and is in heart failure. Regarding timing of valve surgery requiring CPB, which approach is most appropriate?
options: Delay surgery for at least 4 weeks regardless of infarct size
Proceed with early surgery since the infarct is small and the deficit modest
Routinely perform pericardiocentesis before bypass
Avoid heparin entirely and attempt off-pump repair
questions.explanation: After a small, asymptomatic ischemic infarct without hemorrhage, surgery should not be delayed when there is a strong indication such as heart failure from acute MR. A 2-4 week wait is reserved for large or symptomatic infarcts, and ~4 weeks after hemorrhage, balancing hemorrhagic transformation risk from full heparinization.
references: Delgado V et al. 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of endocarditis. Eur Heart J 2023;44:3948-4042.

questions.stem: In elderly patients with severe aortic stenosis referred for TAVR, approximately what proportion harbor occult transthyretin (ATTR) cardiac amyloidosis?
questions.explanation: Roughly 13-16% of elderly patients with severe AS coming for TAVR have occult ATTR amyloid. The amyloid-AS phenotype is typically low-flow, low-gradient with a small, thick, stiff ventricle, and these patients have more conduction disease and pacemaker need.
references: Castano A et al. Unveiling transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis in elderly patients with severe AS undergoing TAVR. Eur Heart J 2017;38:2879-2887.

questions.stem: Which management principle is correct for a patient with cardiac amyloidosis who becomes hypotensive during induction?
options: Administer rapid large-volume fluid boluses as first-line therapy
Treat with vasopressors early; avoid aggressive fluid loading, digoxin, and calcium-channel blockers
Give a calcium-channel blocker to control rate
Induce bradycardia to improve diastolic filling
questions.explanation: The amyloid heart has fixed, rate-dependent output, restrictive filling, and autonomic failure, so hypotension is treated with vasopressors rather than fluid (the stiff ventricle floods into pulmonary edema). Digoxin and CCBs bind amyloid fibrils causing toxicity, and bradycardia directly reduces output.
references: Kittleson MM et al. Cardiac Amyloidosis: Evolving Diagnosis and Management. Circulation 2020;142:e7-e22.

questions.stem: A 99mTc-PYP scan shows grade 3 myocardial uptake in a patient with suspected cardiac amyloidosis. What additional step is required before a non-biopsy diagnosis of ATTR can be made?
options: A positive PYP scan alone is sufficient regardless of other testing
Confirm a negative monoclonal protein screen (serum free light chains plus serum and urine immunofixation)
Perform an endomyocardial biopsy in all cases
Obtain a positive FDG-PET scan
questions.explanation: Grade 2-3 PYP uptake is only 100% specific for ATTR when the monoclonal protein screen is negative. AL amyloid can also take up tracer, and missing AL means missing a rapidly fatal, chemotherapy-treatable disease, so AL must always be excluded first with serum free light chains and serum/urine immunofixation.
references: Gillmore JD et al. Nonbiopsy Diagnosis of Cardiac Transthyretin Amyloidosis. Circulation 2016;133:2404-2412.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old with cardiac sarcoidosis and an ICD presents for surgery. The ICD is not reprogrammed and a magnet is placed over it for electrocautery. What is the key safety consideration?
options: The magnet disables both pacing and tachytherapy, so a pacing-dependent patient is protected
A magnet suspends tachytherapy (shocks) but does NOT disable pacing, so pacing behavior continues
Magnets convert all ICDs to asynchronous pacing reliably
A magnet permanently reprograms the device
questions.explanation: A magnet over most ICDs suspends antitachycardia/shock therapy but does NOT affect bradycardia pacing, which continues in its programmed mode. Assuming the magnet disables pacing is a dangerous error; for a pacing-dependent sarcoid patient, the device should be interrogated, pacing dependence documented, and external pads available.
references: Birnie DH et al. HRS Expert Consensus Statement on Arrhythmias Associated with Cardiac Sarcoidosis. Heart Rhythm 2014;11:1305-1323.

questions.stem: A CT angiogram shows an aortic dissection involving the ascending aorta. By Stanford classification and standard management, what is the appropriate disposition?
options: Stanford type B; medical therapy first
Stanford type A; emergent open surgical repair
Stanford type A; medical therapy first with serial imaging
Stanford type B; emergent TEVAR for all
questions.explanation: Any dissection involving the ascending aorta is Stanford type A and is a surgical emergency requiring emergent open repair on cardiopulmonary bypass. Type B (descending only) is managed medically first and treated invasively only when complicated.
references: Isselbacher EM et al. 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. Circulation 2022;146:e334-e482.

questions.stem: In the initial medical management of acute aortic dissection, what is the correct sequence of anti-impulse therapy?
options: Lower blood pressure first with a vasodilator, then add a beta-blocker
Control heart rate/dP/dt with a beta-blocker first, then add a vasodilator for pressure
Give hydralazine as the first-line agent
Treat pressure and rate simultaneously with nitroprusside alone
questions.explanation: Rate before pressure: beta-blockade to a heart rate ≤60 reduces dP/dt (shear force) first, then a titratable vasodilator (nicardipine, clevidipine, or nitroprusside) is added for a target SBP of 100-120. Vasodilating first causes reflex tachycardia that increases shear; hydralazine is avoided.
references: Isselbacher EM et al. 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. Circulation 2022;146:e334-e482.

questions.stem: A patient with type A dissection arrives hypotensive with JVD and muffled heart sounds; echo shows pericardial effusion with tamponade physiology. Why is routine pericardiocentesis contraindicated?
options: It cannot remove enough fluid to help
Fully relieving tamponade restores ejection force and can precipitate fatal re-rupture
It reliably causes coronary air embolism
Pericardiocentesis is too slow to set up
questions.explanation: In dissection-related hemopericardium, the tamponade itself limits ejection force; fully draining it restores LV ejection pressure against the breached aorta and can precipitate fatal re-rupture. The plan is to prep and drape awake with the surgeon ready to open and go on bypass; if used in extremis, only small controlled volumes are drained as a bridge.
