references: Isselbacher EM et al. 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. Circulation 2022;146:e334-e482.

questions.stem: When setting up arterial monitoring for a type A dissection repair, why is a second arterial line (e.g., femoral and/or left radial in addition to right radial) recommended?
options: To increase the rate of fluid administration
To detect arch-vessel malperfusion and confirm CPB perfusion pressure
Because radial lines are unreliable in dissection
To allow simultaneous cardiac output measurement
questions.explanation: The dissection flap or surgical cannulation/clamping can involve the innominate artery, making a left-sided line read falsely low; a second site (femoral and/or contralateral radial) detects arch-vessel malperfusion and confirms perfusion pressure on bypass. A pulse deficit should be checked first to guide line placement.
references: Augoustides JGT et al. Anesthetic considerations for acute aortic syndromes. In: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed. Elsevier.

questions.stem: Which strategy best protects the spinal cord during TEVAR for a complicated type B dissection?
options: Deliberate hypotension with MAP <60 mmHg throughout
CSF drainage to keep CSF pressure low and maintenance of a generous MAP (often >80-90 mmHg)
Routine hypothermic circulatory arrest
Withholding all blood transfusion to limit volume
questions.explanation: Spinal cord perfusion pressure equals MAP minus CSF pressure, so a lumbar CSF drain (often keeping CSF pressure ≤10 mmHg) plus a generous MAP raises perfusion and reduces paraplegia risk, especially with long stent coverage. Hypotension and anemia increase the risk of anterior spinal cord ischemia.
references: MacGillivray TE et al. STS/AATS Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Type B Aortic Dissection. Ann Thorac Surg 2022;113:1073-1092.

questions.stem: A patient admitted with uncomplicated type B dissection on esmolol and nicardipine develops rising creatinine, oliguria, a lactate of 4.2, and recurrent pain despite controlled blood pressure. What does this represent and what is the next step?
options: Adequate medical control; continue current therapy
A complicated type B (malperfusion plus refractory pain) requiring TEVAR
Expected progression; increase the beta-blocker dose only
An indication for immediate pericardiocentesis
questions.explanation: New renal/mesenteric malperfusion (rising creatinine, oliguria, lactate) plus refractory pain despite good BP control declares the dissection complicated, which is an indication for intervention (usually TEVAR). Continuing medical therapy alone is no longer sufficient.
references: MacGillivray TE et al. STS/AATS Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Type B Aortic Dissection. Ann Thorac Surg 2022;113:1073-1092.

questions.stem: For type A dissection repair requiring arch work, why is axillary/subclavian arterial cannulation generally favored over femoral cannulation?
options: It is faster to establish in an emergency
It delivers antegrade flow into the true lumen and facilitates antegrade cerebral perfusion
It eliminates the need for circulatory arrest
It avoids the need for cerebral oximetry
questions.explanation: Axillary/subclavian cannulation delivers antegrade flow into the true lumen and sets up antegrade cerebral perfusion, the preferred brain-protection strategy. Femoral cannulation is faster but risks retrograde malperfusion of the false lumen and atheroembolism. Circulatory arrest is still needed to open the arch.
references: Isselbacher EM et al. 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. Circulation 2022;146:e334-e482.

questions.stem: A type A dissection extends into the root, causing acute severe aortic insufficiency with pulmonary edema and a non-dilated LV. The patient is tachycardic at 110. Regarding anti-impulse therapy, what is the best approach?
options: Aggressively beta-block to a heart rate of 50 to limit shear
Accept the higher heart rate, since acute AR is rate-dependent and needs forward flow; titrate impulse control cautiously
Add phenylephrine to raise afterload and improve coronary perfusion
Place an intra-aortic balloon pump for hemodynamic support
questions.explanation: With acute AR, the non-compliant LV depends on a faster rate and lower afterload ('full, fast, forward'); aggressive beta-blockade to bradycardia is poorly tolerated and reduces forward flow. Phenylephrine raises afterload and worsens regurgitation, and an IABP is contraindicated in significant AR. Impulse control is balanced against the need for forward flow while expediting surgery.
references: Augoustides JGT et al. Anesthetic considerations for acute aortic syndromes. In: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia. 8th ed. Elsevier.
